behaviors.description: Generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response
behaviors.justification: The real failure is whether the actual LLM, when presented with a multi-point email, produces output that references those specific points or produces boilerplate instead. Mocking the LLM with fixed output always passes, hiding this failure. A mock that returns 'I reference point A' doesn't test whether a real LLM behaves this way. Only a real LLM call reveals if it actually incorporates specific context into the draft.
behaviors.test_description: Provide real LLM with a structured email containing 3+ specific points (e.g., meeting date, client name, action item). Generate draft. Verify draft references all specific points by name, date, or exact term—not generic template text. Repeat with different email structures to verify behavior is consistent.

behaviors.description: Factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base
behaviors.justification: The core failure is: does the checker reliably detect contradictions? If the contradiction detector is rule-based (fact X in KB vs. claim ¬X in draft), Integration level with a real KB is sufficient—mocking KB hides failures where lookup fails or entries have unexpected format. If the checker uses an LLM to evaluate contradictions (more likely for nuanced claims), Agentic level is needed. We assign higher confidence to Integration assuming rule-based matching; significant probability to Agentic if the checker uses LLM evaluation.
behaviors.test_description: Populate knowledge base with concrete facts (e.g., 'Product Y has feature Z', 'Policy limits to $5000'). Generate or provide drafts that make claims directly contradicting KB entries. Run consistency check. Verify: (a) contradictions are flagged, (b) non-contradictory claims are not flagged, (c) partial matches are handled correctly (same fact stated differently).

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts
behaviors.justification: The real failure is LLM hallucination: when the KB is empty or has no relevant entries, does the actual LLM avoid fabricating facts and instead include a disclaimer? Mocking the LLM with 'outputs disclaimer, no fabrication' always passes. Mocking KB as empty doesn't test real LLM behavior. Only running with a real LLM on an empty KB reveals whether it hallucinates or respects the instruction to avoid fabrication.
behaviors.test_description: Clear or mock knowledge base to have no entries relevant to a given topic. Generate draft for that topic using real LLM. Verify: (a) draft includes explicit disclaimer (e.g., 'I could not find this information in the knowledge base'), (b) draft contains no unsourced claims or speculative facts, (c) behavior is consistent across multiple runs.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically
behaviors.justification: This is pure control-flow logic: does the code check approval before sending? The real failure is the approval gate being missing or bypassed entirely. A Unit test mocking the email service can verify that send() is never called unless approval flag is true. Mocking doesn't hide this failure—it's a logic check, not a behavior-dependent check. If approval state persists across sessions, Integration level adds robustness, but the core failure (missing gate) is caught at Unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a draft, attempt to call send() without approval, verify send is blocked (blocked exception or no-op return). Then approve the draft, call send(), verify it succeeds. Test both explicit denial and explicit approval. Verify that no code path allows sending without reaching the approval check.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread
behaviors.justification: This behavior involves multiple agentic steps (LLM-based draft generation and fact checking) in sequence, plus real email I/O and control-flow coordination. The real failure is integration breakdown: email format doesn't parse correctly, LLM output doesn't match fact-checker input format, approval UI doesn't render the draft, or any step silently fails. Mocking every component means the test passes even if real components fail together. Only running the full pipeline with real dependencies and real LLM calls reveals whether the orchestration works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real email thread (or realistic synthetic thread with varied structure, quotes, multiple participants). Run the full pipeline: (1) parse email, (2) generate draft with LLM, (3) extract claims and run fact check, (4) verify draft is presented to approval UI with all metadata intact. Assert no step silently fails, and the flow reaches user approval stage. Verify that output of each step is correctly consumed by the next step.
